8.description: Student sees a 3×4 array (3 horizontal rows of 4 items). When asked "How many rows?", student answers "4." When asked to write the expression for "3 rows of 4," student writes "4 × 3."

9.description: Student sees a 3×4 array and writes "3 + 4 = 7" instead of "3 × 4 = 12."

10.misconception: Groups vs. Size Confusion — Carried from M8
10.description: Student sees 3 rows of 4 and writes "4 × 3" when describing by rows (reversing factors).

13.description: Student sees 3 × 4 = 12 and insists 4 × 3 must be different (or hesitates/recomputes instead of applying the property). When shown a rotated array, student says "that's a different array" or "you can't just switch the numbers." In Exit Check, student correctly identifies both expressions for an array but doesn't connect them to the same product.

20.description: Student can state "you can multiply in any order" but cannot explain why, cannot connect the rule to an array visual, and cannot apply it to unfamiliar facts. Student treats the commutative property as another rule to memorize rather than a structural feature of multiplication they've proven.
